speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. The survivor is Mania Lichtenstein. Today is April 1, 1996. I would like to go back and ask a few more questions about the Judenrat. Can you tell me how many people were on the Judenrat and how they were appointed or selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. All I know, that they existed. And actually they were no great importance. You know, what-- what could they do? They themselves were those who were penned in, you know. But I suppose the purpose was-- was to-- if the Germans needed anything-- any information, or if they needed people for forced labor, as they, you know, used us, as much as they could. And so they would-- they would turn to the Judenrat and ask for so many people they need for a job. And that was actually-- and they just went just when the pogroms came-- they were just murdered the same as anybody else. I mean, they-- they didn't have any special importance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there Judenrat for both ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, there was one-- I don't even remember how many people-- who they were. Well-- all I knew is they needed people to-- through whom to communicate with the-- with the Jews. This was the purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends in this death ghetto that you were sent to? Do you remember playing with anybody, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you did not-- you did not play. Somehow, I don't know-- I myself cannot understand it. You were still young. You were still-- no, you just-- We just sat, as I say. We had to do something. So we-- whatever old sweaters we found, we unravelled. We could not get new wool. And we were knitting-- knitting, singing, and crying. Just-- this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The same songs, in the second ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were so many--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the death ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the second ghetto, we-- [SIGH] No. The-- I'm sorry, that was only in the first ghetto. You know, because the second ghetto, they were nonexistent anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no singing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no singing, there was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: No playing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No playing, no life. [LAUGHS] No, my sisters-- my family was gone in the first ghetto. They all perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were mentioning on the previous tape that you went up on the roof to see what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Being--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us more?

speaker: interviewee
text: The night was so long, and the mood was so depressed. And so we-- so we'll-- we'll run up and see what's happening. And since it was dawn, we were able to see what's happening outside. We looked out the window to see-- is anybody coming? We already saw proof that they were all standing with their rifles, just ready, like on command--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainians and the Germans. And surrounded all to ghetto. We were surrounded. 6 o'clock on the dot-- and this is when-- we were on that upper floor, that we went to see-- visit the other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the morning-- 6 o'clock?

speaker: interviewee
text: 6 O'clock AM. Immediately--

speaker: interviewer
text: On what date? Do you remember what date that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 1, 1942. Immediately, we just saw them surrounding the ghetto. And at 6 o'clock, immediately they dash in the ghetto. We hear shooting and screaming and-- and crying. And there is such chaos that-- that, you know, the whole-- nobody slept. Everybody was up. You know, you-- you can't sleep when you know tomorrow morning--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because these were the rumors. They weren't always-- they weren't always really happening on the days when they predicted, you know? Oh, I think on the 13th-- on the 15th-- you know? But this time, somehow you sensed it. You felt that this is a-- Do you remember what day of the week that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. Well, it wasn't on a weekend. No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened on September 1?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately, we saw action. You know-- screaming and crying. There was no way we can go down to our hiding-- I mean, it wasn't a hiding place. But where we were supposed to be. And we start running to that hiding place on that floor. I say 'we.' I, my sister's in-laws, and they also had, like, the girl that I later on-- we stuck together. Like my sister-in-law's family. [SIGHS] No way could we go down, and so we have to stay on the floor. Because immediately, we heard boots-- the German boots, knocking. And the Ukrainian running in that building in the house, looking for their-- for their prey. And we couldn't go down anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we followed the people from that floor. They had their hiding place in an attic. There was a big-- as I say, it was a prominent family, a rich family-- a closet, with clothes. You know, at that time, they-- the clothes was an armoire, you know? A little French-- where you kept your clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the back wall of  that closet, they made sliding. And behind that sliding wall of that closet was the entrance to an attic. And this is where our hiding place. We went with these people to that place. Immediately-- because, as I say, already you heard the Germans and the-- and the Ukrainian running, and you could hear what's going on. And immediately, we heard my-- my little friend's mother being taken out. Because her mother-- she-- she had a little sister and a baby. The baby was maybe six months old or something like this. And immediately, you heard them moaning and-- oy, oy, and crying, and the baby-- because the baby started crying. And this was always the case. Wherever there was a baby, the hiding place was immediately exposed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sit upstairs, and my friend. And we hear her mother crying. Oy, oy, you know, they're leading them out of-- to be shot. And we can't do nothing. We just sit there. It was so funny. [SIGH] Strange. How-- how your mind-- like, nothing mattered, you know? Everything that's happening because it has to happen. And those that were dead already were the lucky ones. It's so quiet. It's so peaceful. Because you're still waiting for it, you know? So let them get over with it, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lucky were the ones that were gone. They have peace, and-- now, I can see it as peace. But then-- it's so quiet, there-- nobody bothers you. I don't know. It's-- it's just my way of-- the way I perceive it-- perceived it, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you looking forward to your own death, at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it didn't matter. You-- you knew it-- avoid-- you can't escape it. So, lucky the ones who were-- are beyond this already. It's over. You know? You never even thought that-- how? How will you escape? Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were hiding in this space?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that space-- it was an attic, you know? You might not maybe know the-- the way the structures was a building. Every house had an attic. A loft, I suppose, you'd call it. But here it's-- I don't know. An attic. If you look at the old-fashioned, maybe, pictures. It was-- it was quite big. And we were about maybe 15 people in there. Whoever was on the floor ran to that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we were sitting-- it was already September 1. The sun was blazing. It was hot. And it was a tin roof, right above our heads. And we were just sitting-- crouched, you say? You know, not talking, not-- just listening was-- to the horror outside, we were listening.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] immediately they started tearing apart every Jewish house. The attics, the-- every-- every place that they would suspect that you might be hiding. Oh, they were-- they were searching for gold, you know, to-- to find the prey. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And we heard them stomp on our-- on our roof. And then the Ukrainian-- I won't say it in Ukrainian-- you won't understand-- but stomping and saying, do you think we will find Jews here? And they stomp. They are looking for gold.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we sit. And-- and they banged on our heads. And they were tearing apart every house, because it was so important to find-- they had a contingent of-- they had to produce 19,000 this time-- 19,000 Jews were supposed to perish in that pogrom. As I say, Germans are very-- precision is the most important thing. 19,000, and that's it. The rest will be for the future. And we sit, and they-- and we hear them, banging and looking. But somehow, as I say, fate-- they do not tear our roof apart. Because they already found all the other hiding in-- on the floors. They didn't even have a chance to really hide, immediately, as they--

speaker: interviewer
text: If you hadn't looked out the window and saw them, standing, ready with their rifles, knowing what it means, you wouldn't even know that they are there. You know? It was quiet. It was night.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we happened to look out, and we realized that, yeah, it's starting. And we sit, day after day after day. We hear screaming. We hear shooting. We hear crying. Because the action lasted 15 days. 15 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how long you were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19,000! That was-- even for the Germans, it wasn't easy to-- you know? And, after all, people were not standing exposed and-- come get me! They hid-- 19 days-- 15, excuse me, 15 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, during that time, you were--?

speaker: interviewee
text: During that time, there's no food, there is no water.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're in the attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the attic. The sun-- the hot tin roof is [SIGH] blazing on your hair. You don't move. You-- you just sit, lest you-- you-- you make a [INAUDIBLE], you would give yourself out. We didn't have any babies, you know, so-- if we had a baby, no way could we survive. So, all grown people. But slowly-- you-- you didn't need-- it's amazing. You wouldn't believe it. We had no food. There was a sack of peas and raw potatoes in there, so we nibbled on the raw potatoes. But the food wasn't important, even. You-- I-- I can't understand it. I feel I'm imagining or I'm exaggerating. There was no food. No food. 15 days, a man can survive without food. But on the eighth day, without water, and it's hot, people-- Some-- one man got berserk-- started screaming. But less and less, you-- we would hear them banging and looking, because they covered already this area. So now they look somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the man got berserk and start screaming. You know, you became a little berserk. You can't-- one thing-- I don't have to be a scientist to know it. You cannot survive without water. A drink of water--

speaker: interviewee
text: We just prayed, God, let them come and get us. But give us a drink of water before they shoot us. It is so-- I-- yeah, as you know, I don't have to convince you that, without water, there is no life. And we could not take it any longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a young boy hiding with us, a teenager. He remembered on one of the balconies a dish of rainwater. You know, you usually tried to salvage rainwater. It was soft. You washed your hair with it, you know? Not for drinking.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he remembered that, on one of these balconies, there is a dish with rainwater. And that was the eighth day. And he sacrificed to go out, fetched that water-- in the building-- in the building. It wasn't going outside. But then again, without the water we are finished, anyway. So it-- we had to-- it didn't matter. He went, and he brought back that dish with water. And we all took a sip.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you determine who would take the first sip?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I'll tell you what. My sister's father-in-law-- very distinguished old man. Or maybe he wasn't. Maybe he was younger than I am now, you know? But, you know, when you're young, and a man with a white beard-- So even your parent-- my parents were young, but they're your parents, so they're old, you know. I don't know who took the first-- but my sister's father-in-law, when it came to his-- his turn to have a drink, he said, give it to the children. And-- and-- we-- and it was given to me and to my little friend, who was a relative of them. He-- But I'll soon-- I'll soon tell you what the consequences were, after that 15 days. By that was-- wasn't it, like, noble-- I just remember him saying, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Let the children have it. Like he didn't matter. And everybody got a drink of that stale, yucky water that you wouldn't even dare to drink now. What water meant for me, from that day on, I must say, now-- I-- I do remember, now and then, but not always. But for so, so many years after the war, every time I would go to that sink and open up the-- and-- and get water, and I say, for goodness sake! People just don't realize what they possess! Anytime they feel like it, they just turn the faucet and they drink water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Water-- [SIGH] I just can't explain. Even now, it seem, was it, really? But how do you live without-- without food and without water? And the sun is-- the sun is baking you. You're baked in the sun, and no water. All we wanted-- take us out already, but give us a drink of water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, 15 days. We look out, you know, every day. In the meantime-- I must-- this I must say. In the meantime, this was in the dead ghetto, where I came-- they brought me in the dead ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, we looked through the-- because the attic was wooden planks, like. You know? So in between the-- what do you call it? Between the-- what do you call it? Like, the cracks between-- we look out, to see what's happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: You could see the living ghetto, and it's an inferno. The whole ghetto's on fire. What happened there-- because they were the living ghetto. It's all tricks, you know, which eased their way to accomplish what they wanted to. When-- when-- when the dead ghetto-- you know, like, it took 15 days, but, of course, 19,000 were taken, put on trucks, and brought to the mass graves which were ready, waiting for them. And these graves-- like, a few weeks, maybe-- two, three weeks before, Jews were taken-- like, this was another forced labor-- taken to dig-- to dig graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they called it-- they say they are preparing for an airport. What airport will be needed in our city? It was for an airport-- ein Flugplatz. This is what-- But, of course, Jewish intuition-- they knew what-- what-- they knew what-- what it meant. So they were mass graves. 19,000 were buried in one-- gigantic. I mean, they took-- labor was cheap, right? So they took as many men as-- and-- and 19,000 were buried in that grave. Now, in the-- in the living ghetto, they saw these trucks, passing by, loaded like cattle, like sardines, you know, packed as-- the more the better, you know? Bringing them to [INAUDIBLE], that place where the mass graves stood.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was a town that almost I might not have known-- I knew my friends, my school friends, my neighbors. But I'm sure all the people knew-- almost everybody. And they saw them-- and relatives-- passing by, you know, the cattle, taking them to the graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, of course, you can imagine their situation. They were crying. They were screaming. They knew-- they knew what's happening, and it's not just to the people that are on the-- in the trucks. They knew this is it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans kept telling them [GERMAN]. You have no angst-- no-- no no fright. Have no fright! You are the living ghetto. Like they are  going to their death-- or wherever they are going-- but you remain alive, because you are the living ghetto. And they kind of did not hide anymore. They were just standing and crying. Everybody saw an aunt, a-- maybe a daughter, maybe a son, maybe-- I mean, everybody had everybody. It was one city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were just standing and crying. And, of course, to my family, I am already-- where am I? Where am I? I'm not here. So I'm somewhere in the trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and nobody was hiding. And exactly at 1 o'clock, they ambushed the ghetto and had them all like-- like you pick up-- and this was the living ghetto. Immediately, they were taken also on the trucks and to the graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this also happened on September 1?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that day. Very organized. I mean-- perfection. So the living ghetto was exterminated-- finished-- non-- not existing anymore. Now, I am not aware-- I mean, I can imagine, but I'm not-- I don't know what's happening with my family. I'm here, and I see the inferno. Everything is a ball of fire. Because they immediately burned up the whole thing. I guess-- no-- no proof, no-- it's-- it's clean. Erased all the-- not really, but erased the evidence, kind of. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and we are just-- and this was happening-- this was not on the 15th day, this-- immediately, in the beginning. We just sit there in that attic, and we don't know what-- will it ever end? What happens next? No idea. But on the 15th days-- 15th day, we look out through the cracks, and we see Germans, and we see a group of people, you know, walking like-- like skeletons, like-- couldn't be any-- anybody else but Jews, you know? The pogrom ended. And-- and those remaining-- they weren't going to exterminate all, only 19,000-- Germans, it has to be-- even if would be 19,001, no good. 19,000. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And immediately, we could tell they are Jews. Are they taking them to work, I don't know. But you could see, like, watching like-- like-- bent, no life, no-- no soul in them. Like they just-- vegetables.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we said, hey, this looks like it's over, you know? So, slowly-- you didn't hear shooting anymore, crying, screaming. There was no chaos. Quiet. And slowly, we started-- we-- we could tell for sure it's Jews. And we saw more coming out. So we decided that it must be over, after 15 days. During the 15 days, you mentioned there was one day that you were able to get water. This brave young man--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --risked his life, in a way, to bring you water. Was that the only time that you had water during those 15 days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only time. Only time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, for your personal needs, you know, we were just doing in front of the others, you know, in a corner. Because there were no facilities, you know, to go. But, you know, lucky, nobody needed to go. Nobody--

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't eat, you didn't drink, you know? So that was a blessing in disguise. But it was so embarrassing, you-- you know. And there was no food, of course?

speaker: interviewee
text: No food. The raw potato-- funny how you survive without food. You didn't need any. You know, food-- you didn't think of food. Honestly!

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about at night, when you were sleeping?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We were not human beings. No, we just-- you're surviving, surviving, like-- hiding. You-- you didn't-- the end result, you didn't care what if you survive or how-- how on earth can you think of surviving, where there is no escape?

speaker: interviewer
text: We know that it has-- why-- why kill the others? Why kill my mother and my father, my sisters, and anybody else, and not me? What am I, chosen to-- So we knew there was no-- I don't know what we were thinking. Just-- just looking-- Was there any conversation?

speaker: interviewee
text: If there was, it was-- first of all, you had no strength to talk. You have no food. When you tried, to, let's say, to stand up, to attend to your human needs, the dress was hanging.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, I wish I can lose, uh-- [LAUGH] something, now, that there I lost like-- didn't need to go on a diet. On a special diet. Everybody became a skeleton. You couldn't walk. Like, when you walked, you had to hold on. There was no energy, you know? So you mentioned, after 15 days, it seemed safe to come out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we saw already people-- ants crawling. So we come out and realize that the pogrom is over. Now, they are preparing a smaller-- Actually, it was where the dead ghetto they put us-- in dead ghetto. Living ghetto, no more-- finished. Now it's only the dead ghetto they put us. There were 7,000 left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of how many, to begin with?

speaker: interviewee
text: 26,000. 19,000, then 7,000. Immediately they set up a kitchen to-- I don't know what. I don't even remember what they-- Immediately we started roaming. The dead peoples-- I mean, every house was empty. There were-- you know, from this amount to 7,000, every house, almost-- every house was empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we found a little flour. Actually, I-- I didn't know how to cook. I just never did at home. But my little friend-- you had to be daring. I mean, how do you know how to bake a bread or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: She took the flour, she mixed some water, and she baked it. Already we had something to eat. Whatever we could find-- because there wasn't much to be found, after what I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Exhausted.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your first food that you had, after 15 days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I suppose that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: --that bread that she baked-- the water and flour. She mixed it and she baked it. But what happened-- was very little food. But, of course, we drank water. We drank water-- And what happened is we got such diarrhea that you didn't want to eat anymore. And people were just dying. My-- my sister's father-in-law, he just lied down and died. Young people-- the ones that I was hiding with-- there were quite a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the gentleman that passed the water--

speaker: interviewee
text: The water, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the other one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Just lied down, and he was no more. And young people. At that time, I must say, I-- I didn't understand the-- the-- the sense of it. Why should they die?

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, I was very strong. I never had a cold. I was always unhappy I could never miss a day of school, because I wasn't sick, You know? But actually, whatever, every system responds differently. They just-- I couldn't understand. Why are they dying? Just lying down, and they're no more. Right after we came out from that 15 days of hiding. What I understand now is dehydration. You know? You can only take so long, you know, until you dry up and become driftwood. What would happen to the dead bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we some-- I don't know. I am not very tough, you know, like-- I suppose I wouldn't have anything to do with it, physically, to-- to do it. I suppose they buried them wherever possible. It didn't mean anything, because this was only-- only a short act. You know? You had to go. You had to do-- to the bigger acts. This was like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any special prayers that were offered, when you came across someone that died, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was there to-- no who was there to offer? There was nobody. [INAUDIBLE] Morgenstern-- Morgenstern was dead, like, with the first ones. There was nobody. And also there were hardly any older people-- or babies. Babies, little kids-- no more. Because for them it was immediately-- they were taken out. And older people, they just succumbed, you know. They could not-- and when you see your children killed--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was one mother, and she saw her children shot in front of her eyes. But she became insane. And she was killed in the next pogrom. But it was a terrible thing, you know? How did you know that these people that were selected, the first 19,000, were killed and buried in a mass grave? How did you personally know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they weren't selected. They were caught like stray dogs, as I say in my story. Caught like stray dogs. You come and you catch whoever you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: We knew about the graves. We knew about the trucks-- that they were-- we knew that they were taken there. They were no more. Where did they go-- on vacation? We knew that they-- they are gone. They are no more. Did you hear the shooting? I mean, was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we couldn't hear--

speaker: interviewer
text: The mass graves were far from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like 12 kilometer-- they wouldn't do it in the middle of the town, even though they couldn't care less. But listen, a little discretion. Even a Pole might maybe sometimes-- witness. You know? Why have witnesses? So-- ghetto. A new ghetto, with 7,000. A kitchen opens. They give us coffee or the soup. And, again, we get-- how do you say it-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put us and place us-- you live here. People-- we were, like, five girls-- young girls-- living in a little cubicle.
